items.q: What happens to electrons when sodium reacts with chlorine?
items.correct: Sodium loses one electron to chlorine; the resulting Na+ and Cl- ions attract electrostatically.
items.misconception: Sodium and chlorine share an electron between them.
items.random_wrong: Protons move from chlorine to sodium to balance the charges.
items.why: Students over-apply the covalent (sharing) model to all bonding because sharing is introduced first and feels intuitive.
It's Salt + water. “Just water” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which salt is made from hydrochloric acid + sodium hydroxide?
items.why: Correct answer: Sodium chloride. “Sodium sulfate” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If you mix equal amounts of pH 3 acid with pH 11 alkali of equal strength, what should the final pH be approximately?
items.why: The real figure is 7. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
